coredata.dc:title: A facile green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots using Pumpkin seeds for ultra-sensitive Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions detection in living cells

Bhuvanalogini, Govindaraju
coredata.dc:description: A facile and efficient green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) was achieved using pumpkin seeds (PS) through the hydrothermal-carbonization method. Pumpkin seeds, well-known for their nutrient-rich composition, offer potential health benefits, including heightened fertility, enhanced heart health, and improved blood sugar regulation. The as-synthesized PS-CDs characterization through UV–vis, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, XRD, and HR(TEM) analysis. The PL PS-CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior, with the peak shifting from 410 nm to 475 nm under excitation wavelengths ranging from 270 nm to 400 nm. Notably, the synthesized PS-CDs displayed outstanding advantages, such as a high fluorescent quantum yield of 12.7 % with bright blue emissions when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. High-resolution TEM images revealed an average size of approximately 4.0 ± 0.2 nm for the synthesized PS-CDs, ranging from 2 to 7 nm with interlayer distance of 0.21 nm. PL spectra of PS-CDs solution, with an excitation wavelength of 338 nm and emission wavelength of 420 nm, were examined in the presence of various metal ions (50 μM). The fluorescence lifetime of the PS-CDs showed an exponential curve, with an average lifetime (τav) of 8.47 ns for Cu2+and 3.61 ns for Fe3+ ions, respectively, which is strongly indicates that quenching occurs through a static mechanism. The PS-CDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, with a linear relationship observed up to a concentration of 50 μM and detection limits of 0.43 and 0.11 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. Moreover, the low-toxicity and strongly fluorescent PS-CDs were successfully applied for cell imaging. The practical utility of the developed sensor was determined through the analysis of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in spiked water samples, highlighting its considerable analytical and bioanalytical applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of hydrothermal synthesis of PS-CDs from pumpkin seeds and its metal ions sensing.

(a) UV−vis, PL excitation, and emission spectra of the PS-CDs in aqueous solution; inset: photographs of photoluminescent PS-CDs solution under daylight and UV light at 365 nm. (b) The photoluminescence emission spectra of PS-CDs aqueous solution under the different excitation from 270 to 430 nm.
PS-CDs stability of (a) pH and (b) NaCl. The corresponding photographs of pH (c) and NaCl (d) under daylight and UV light at 365 nm.
The effects of various conditions on the PL intensity of PS-CDs; (a) radiation time and (b) storage time.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD pattern of PS-CDs, (c) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of PS-CDs, and (d) Particle size distributions of PS-CDs.
XPS spectra of PS-CDs (a) survey spectrum with high-resolution spectra of C1s (b), O1s (c), and N1s (d).

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of PS-CDs solution with excitation wavelength at 338 nm and emission wavelength at 420 nm in the presence of various metal ions (50 μM), (b) Relative photoluminescence intensity of PS-CDs solution in the presence of various metal ions (50 μM). (c) The corresponding photographs of PS-CDs solution in the presence of various metal ions (50 μM) under daylight and UV light at 365 nm.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the PS-CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of Cu2+ ions from 0 to 50 μM, (b) Jobs plot of Cu2+ ions, (c) Relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Cu2+ ions, and (d) Intensity profile of Cu2+ ions.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the PS-CDs upon the addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions from 0 to 50 μM, (b) Jobs plot of Fe3+ ions, (c) Relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+ ions, and (d) Intensity profile of Fe3+ ions.
Interference effect of the relative photoluminescence intensity of PS-CDs solution with 50 μM of (a) Cu2+ and (b) Fe3+ ions in the presence of various metal ions (100 μM).
Lifetime profiles of (a) chemosensors PS-CDs in the presence of Cu2+ ions and (b) chemosensors PS-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively.

(a) Cell toxicity of the PS-CDs with HCT-116 cells (b). Confocal microscopic bright-field, fluorescence, and overlay images of HCT-116 cells incubated with PS-CDs, PS-CDs/Cu2+, and PS-CDs/Fe3+ for 24 h. Scale bar∼100 µm.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and efficient green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) was achieved using pumpkin seeds (PS) through the hydrothermal-carbonization method. Pumpkin seeds, well-known for their nutrient-rich composition, offer potential health benefits, including heightened fertility, enhanced heart health, and improved blood sugar regulation. The as-synthesized PS-CDs characterization through UV–vis, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, XRD, and HR(TEM) analysis. The PL PS-CDs exhibited an excitation-dependent behavior, with the peak shifting from 410 nm to 475 nm under excitation wavelengths ranging from 270 nm to 400 nm. Notably, the synthesized PS-CDs displayed outstanding advantages, such as a high fluorescent quantum yield of 12.7 % with bright blue emissions when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. High-resolution TEM images revealed an average size of approximately 4.0 ± 0.2 nm for the synthesized PS-CDs, ranging from 2 to 7 nm with interlayer distance of 0.21 nm. PL spectra of PS-CDs solution, with an excitation wavelength of 338 nm and emission wavelength of 420 nm, were examined in the presence of various metal ions (50 μM). The fluorescence lifetime of the PS-CDs showed an exponential curve, with an average lifetime (τav) of 8.47 ns for Cu2+and 3.61 ns for Fe3+ ions, respectively, which is strongly indicates that quenching occurs through a static mechanism. The PS-CDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, with a linear relationship observed up to a concentration of 50 μM and detection limits of 0.43 and 0.11 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. Moreover, the low-toxicity and strongly fluorescent PS-CDs were successfully applied for cell imaging. The practical utility of the developed sensor was determined through the analysis of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in spiked water samples, highlighting its considerable analytical and bioanalytical applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, a novel addition to the nanocarbon family, have garnered significant consideration in recent research owing to their distinctive chemical and optical properties [1]. These nanoparticles stand out from conventional small molecules and quantum dots due to their low toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility, and steady photoluminescence [2]. Due to these properties, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles have become highly sought after in various fields, including bio-labeling, sensing, catalysis, and biomedicine. Biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) are particularly promising due to their high fluorescent emissions and stability, presenting versatile applications across multiple domains [3]. The choice of carbon sources is crucial in CD preparation, and biomass carbon sources from the natural world offer advantages such as affordability, non-toxicity, and abundance. Several methods for biomass CD synthesis have been proposed, including the hydrothermal method [4–10], microwave method [11], microwave method [12], chemical oxidation method [13], hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) method [14], and pyrolysis carbonization method [15]. However, these techniques often come with drawbacks such as multistep fabrication, low yield, complex setups, time-intensive processes, high temperatures, post-separation steps, and elevated costs.

The green synthesis of CDs, utilizing natural and renewable carbon precursors, has emerged as a compelling research area [16]. Exploring green sources for CDs is particularly exciting, given their affordability, cleanliness, nontoxic nature, and easy accessibility. Among the various methods, hydrothermal synthesis has become a popular choice for CDs synthesis due to its simplicity, eco-friendliness, control over size and purity, low cost, and non-toxicity compared to alternative techniques [17]. Researchers have explored numerous biomass carbon sources for CDs synthesis, including sesame, apple, fennel, capsicum, Acacia concinna seeds [18–22], rose flowers [23], ginkgo fruits [24], watermelon juice [25], ginkgo leaves [26], and Trapa bispinosa peel [27], showcasing applications in ions detection, bio-imaging, electrocatalysis, and electroluminescence. Notably, pumpkin seeds (PS), recognized for their high nutritional value and potential health benefits, including improved fertility, enhanced heart health, and better blood sugar control, offer an attractive option [28]. Protein-rich PS, with a dry matter content ranging from 24.5 to 36 %, have drawn attention because they are rich in antioxidants like beta carotene and selenium as well as polyunsaturated fatty acids [29]. These proteins, which include important amino acids like isoleucine, histidine, phenylalanine, and leucine, offer a viable and affordable option for use as food ingredients, especially after partial hydrolysis [29]. Consequently, PS was selected as the first carbon precursor without the need to modify or functionalize using the hydrothermal treatment method.

The industrial evolution accompanying the growth of the human population results in the release of substantial quantities of heavy metals and transition metal ions, significantly impacting environmental safety and human health [30]. Consequently, researchers are actively pursuing the development of a fluorescent sensor that exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of heavy metal ions [31]. Of these metal ions, ferric ion (Fe3+) is of special importance since it is the most abundant metal cation in nature and one of the most important transition metals in biological systems and living organisms [32]. Fe3+ plays a key role in many biological processes, such as electron transport, oxygen exchange and transport, catalysis, and enzymatic reactions within cells [33]. In a similar vein, copper ion (Cu2+) is a common transition metal that the human body needs trace amounts of. It is essential for the growth and operation of blood, the immune system, the central nervous system, hair, and many other human tissues and organs [34]. Cu2+ ion is the second essential trace element, needed in an adult body at a total concentration of about 100–150 mg to maintain normal physiological functions [35]. On the other hand, Alzheimer's disease, cancer, and Parkinson's syndrome have all been connected to the buildup of excess iron and copper in the human body through food chains [36]. Conventional methods for identifying Cu2+ and Fe3+ions include voltammetry, ion-selective membrane electrodes, ICP-MS AAS. These methods are labor-intensive, complicated, and frequently require the use of costly or hazardous reagents [37–39]. Moreover, these methods typically require sophisticated and costly instrumentation, along with laborious sample treatment procedures. As a result of their simplicity and ability to analyze samples with minimal preparation, fluorescent techniques have become popular for detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in biological and environmental systems [7,8]. Consequently, there is a compelling need to explore carbon dots (CDs) with high selectivity and sensitivity for sensing Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions.

In this study, pumpkin seeds (PS) were utilized as precursors for the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) through a straightforward and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method, devoid of any passivates or additives (Scheme 1 ). The resultant PS-CDs demonstrated exceptional photoluminescence (PL) properties, good water-solubility, acceptable quantum yield, low toxicity, and demonstrated good biocompatibility. Characterization of the PS-CDs was conducted through UV–vis, PL, FTIR, XRD, and HR(TEM) analyses. The PS-CDs solution exhibited blue PL upon UV light radiation and displayed a PL "turn-off" sensing to Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, demonstrating high selectivity and sensitivity. A robust linear relationship was observed within the concentration (0–50 μM), and the LOD were 0.43 and 0.11 μM for Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, respectively. These findings suggest significant potential for the PS-CDs as a novel PL probe for consistent, visual, and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. Moreover, its outstanding optical characteristics, PS-CDs were successfully applied to image Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in HCT-116 cells. This further emphasizes the versatility and applicability of PS-CDs in biological imaging, indicating promising prospects for their use in advanced sensing and imaging technologies.

The synthesis of PS-CDs thorough a hydrothermal treatment process. In the standard procedure, Pumpkin seeds (PS) an initial treatment, which included washing with ultrapure water and subsequent desiccation in a precision oven for 5 h at 100 °C. Following this, the dried PS were precisely crushed into a fine powder. Subsequently, 1.0 g of this powdered PS material was introduced into a Teflon-lined autoclave hydrothermal system, along with 25.0 mL of H2O. The entire system was then heated for a duration of 18 h at a temperature of 200 °C. After the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was gradually cooled to room temperature, and the resulting carbon crude was subjected to another filtration process using a 0.45 μm syringe filter. In order to obtain ultra-small PS-CDs, the filtered material underwent a dialysis process with a molecular cutoff of 12,000–14,000 Da against Milli-Q purified water at 9 h using a dialysis membrane. The resulting solution was then stored at 4 °C for subsequent characterization.

To optimize the test conditions, the effect of pH and ionic strength on the photoluminescence (PL) of the PS-CDs was examined. Various pH levels were investigated by diluting PS-CDs with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH (1 to 13) for measurement. Additionally, to assess the effect of ionic strength, different concentrations of NaCl upto 1.0 M were introduced into the PS-CDs solution.

The selectivity of PS-CDs was evaluated in the occurrence of interfering cations, such as Ag+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ce3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zr2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ir3+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Na+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ under identical conditions. The PS-CDs solution was utilized to detect each metal ion at a predetermined concentration of 50 μM. More specifically, HEPES buffer solution was used to detect Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions at room temperature. As per the standard protocol, the PS-CDs solution was prepared by mixing 2.8 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with 200 μL of the aqueous solution containing PS-CDs (50 μg/mL) in a quartz cuvette in spectrophotometer. The cuvette was then filled with 10 μL of a stock solution containing known concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions (0 to 50 μM). After that, the cuvette was shaken for 5 min to make sure the combination was uniform. The prepared PS-CDs solution (50 μg/mL) was mixed with a solution containing a given concentration of each metal ions for the selectivity test using PL spectra in the wavelength range of 350–600 nm with excitation at 338 nm.

Pumpkin seeds were utilized as the exclusive carbon source in a one-pot hydrothermal treatment process to prepared photoluminescent (PL) PS-CDs. This environmentally friendly approach utilized a readily available, cost-effective raw material containing essential elements such as Zinc, Manganese, protein fiber, polyunsaturated fatty acids, and antioxidants like selenium and beta carotenes, along with high levels of iron. Following the hydrothermal treatment, UV–Vis and PL spectral analyses were presented to study the optical characteristics of the PS-CDs. In the UV–Vis spectra of the PS-CDs aqueous solution (Fig. 1 a), a distinct peak at 280 nm and a shoulder peak around 310 nm were observed. These peaks are attributed to the π–π* electron transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the creation of excited defect surface states, contributing to robust PL emission [40]. The maximum excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths for PS-CDs were determined as 338 and 420 nm, respectively. The PS-CDs aqueous solution appears colorless and transparent in daylight but emits a bright blue PL under UV light at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 1a). In Fig. 1b, the PL emission spectrum of the PS-CDs under various excitation wavelength (270 to 430 nm) is illustrated. The emission wavelength exhibited a red-shift with increasing excitation wavelength. The emission intensity showed an initial increase from 270 nm to 340 nm, followed by a gradual decrease up to 430 nm [41]. This behavior suggests that the PL properties of PS-CDs are influenced by the nanoparticle size and color, possibly due to surface states. The excitation-dependent PL characteristics are indicative of diverse surface defects and states in the PS-CDs [41].

The investigation into the PL stability of PS-CDs showing several key factors, including pH, salt ionic strength, radiation time, and storage time. To assess stability, the PL intensity of PS-CDs was evaluated using a fluorescence spectrophotometer [42]. The PL intensity of PS-CDs solutions was studied across a wide pH range from 1 to 13 (Fig. 2 a). The results indicate that there is a notable and robust PL intensity within the physiological pH range of 5 to 7. This observation suggests that the electronic transitions involved in the PL process, specifically the π−π* transitions [43], are influenced by changes in pH. These transitions are related to the refilling or depletion of valence bands, which are affected by the protonation or deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of the PS-CDs. The impact of ionic strength, specifically the concentration of NaCl solutions on the PL of PS-CDs was also investigated. Various NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.2 M to 1.0 M were used as solvents for the PS-CDs (Fig. 2b). Interestingly, the PL intensity showed minimal changes despite variations in NaCl concentration [44]. This stability in PL intensity across different ionic strengths suggests that the PL properties of PS-CDs are not significantly affected by changes in the surrounding ionic environment. This stability is advantageous for potential applications of PS-CDs, as it ensures consistent PL performance regardless of environmental conditions. Additionally, the optical properties of PS-CDs were further characterized by observing their optical peak under both daylight and UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. 2c,d). This characterization likely involved visual inspection or spectroscopic analysis to identify any changes in the emission spectrum or intensity of the PS-CDs under different lighting conditions.

The PL intensity of PS-CDs showed only a slight decrease, maintaining approximately 94 % of its initial intensity after 30 min of radiation (Fig. 3 a). This observation suggests that PS-CDs are resilient to the effects of radiation, which could induce chemical or physical changes in the material. The ability of PS-CDs to retain their optical properties under such conditions is indicative of their robustness and suitability for applications where exposure to radiation is a concern. Similarly, the PL intensity of PS-CDs exhibited only a slight decrease even after 90 days of storage (Fig. 3b). This finding underscores the long-term stability of PS-CDs, indicating minimal degradation or alteration of their optical properties over extended periods. Such stability is crucial for practical applications that require reliable performance over time, such as in sensor devices or imaging applications. These results collectively emphasize the excellent stability of PS-CDs, making them well-suited for various applications in PL sensing and imaging. The consistent PL intensity over prolonged radiation and storage times enhances their utility in diverse environments and under different experimental conditions. Moreover, the favorable optical properties exhibited by PS-CDs, coupled with their stability, suggest promising prospects for their use in biological systems. The ability of PS-CDs to maintain their optical characteristics makes them attractive candidates for biomedical applications, where reliability and consistency are paramount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of PS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural, morphological, and elemental compositions of PS-CDs were comprehensively characterized through FTIR, XRD, TEM (HRTEM), and XPS analyses. The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 4 a sheds light on PS-CDs surface functional groups. The O–H and N–H stretching vibrations were found to be responsible for strong bands at 3348 and 2930 cm–1, respectively, indicating the presence of these functional groups [45]. Furthermore, an absorption peak at 1404 cm–1 indicated the stretching vibration of C = N, while a weak characteristic peak at 1624 cm–1 aligned with the stretching vibration of C = C. The CN-C and CO-C stretching vibrations were linked to the sharp bands at 1106 and 1021 cm–1, respectively [46], indicating that the synthesized PS-CDs contained a significant number of functional groups. As shown in Fig. 4b, the XRD pattern of the PS-CDs revealed a distinct and intense broad peak around 2θ = 20.3°, corresponding to the (002) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon [47]. Noteworthy is the considerably low degree of crystallization, reflected in the weaker intensity of the diffraction peak compared to conventional graphene materials. This observation underscores the unique structural characteristics of the synthesized PS-CDs.

The morphology of the PS-CDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), providing detailed insights into their structure and characteristics. TEM analysis revealed the morphology of the PS-CDs, as depicted in Fig. 4c. This imaging technique allows for the visualization of nanoscale structures with high resolution. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging enabled the identification of the graphitic carbon lattice plane (100). The inset in Fig. 4c shows an HRTEM image where a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane was observed [24]. This finding provides valuable information about the crystalline structure of the PS-CDs, indicating the presence of ordered carbon arrangements within the nanoparticles. The analysis revealed that the PS-CDs had an average particle size of 4.0 ± 0.2 nm, with a narrow size distribution ranging from 2.0 to 7.0 nm. This uniformity in particle size suggests a controlled synthesis process, which is essential for achieving consistent properties and performance in applications. Fig. 4d shows a homogeneous dispersion of the PS-CDs without obvious aggregation. This observation confirms the high-quality particle properties of the produced PS-CDs, indicating that they maintain their individual integrity and do not clump together, which is crucial for their functionality and application performance. Overall, the TEM analysis provides a comprehensive characterization of the morphology and structure of the PS-CDs, revealing their nanoscale dimensions, crystalline properties, and dispersion quality. The combination of FTIR, XRD, and TEM analyses offers a detailed characterization of the structural and morphological attributes of the PS-CDs, providing valuable insights into their composition and quality. These findings lay the groundwork for a more profound understanding and utilization of the unique properties exhibited by the synthesized PS-CDs.

The surface functional groups and elemental states of PS-CDs were subjected to detailed characterization using XPS analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 5 . The XPS survey spectrum of PS-CDs, presented in Fig. 5a, exhibited three distinct and intense peaks at 285.2, 400.3, and 531.2 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5b, four discernible peaks at 283.3, 284.5, 285.8, and 287.5 eV were identified, originating from the C = C, COH/CC, CN, and C = O/C = N groups, respectively [21]. This detailed breakdown sheds light on the diverse carbon bonding configurations present in the PS-CDs. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) further revealed two distinct peaks at 531.4 and 532.5 eV, attributed to C = O and COH/CO-C groups, respectively [25].

This analysis provides valuable information regarding the oxygen-containing group on PS-CDs surface, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of their chemical composition. In addition, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) exhibited two prominent peaks at 398.9 eV, 401.8 eV, and 406.3 eV, corresponding to the CN-C, NH and NO bands, respectively [22]. This insight into the nitrogen states within the PS-CDs offers crucial information about the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups, contributing to the overall surface chemistry of the synthesized material. The XPS analysis, with its high sensitivity and resolution, serves as a powerful tool to elucidate the intricate surface chemistry of PS-CDs. The identified peaks and corresponding functional groups provide a detailed molecular fingerprint, essential for understanding the surface composition and tailoring the properties of these nanostructures for specific applications.

The potential application of PS-CDs as an active electron acceptor for precise detection of metal ion, facilitated by electron transfer with metal ions [48], has been investigated. In this context, we conducted an evaluation of the selectivity of PS-CDs toward common cations (50 μM), including Ag+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ce3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zr2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ir3+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Na+, Sn2+, and Zn2+in living organisms. The results, depicted in Fig. 6 a, revealed a distinctive response compared to other designated species, only Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions induced an important PL turn-off phenomenon [49]. Fig. 6b further illustrates the relative PL intensity of the PS-CDs solution in the presence of various metal ions (50 μM). Notably, Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions caused a substantial PL quenching, suggesting that PS-CDs could serve as a turn-off chemosensor for the selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions [49]. The visual impact of metal ion interactions with PS-CDs is demonstrated in Fig. 6c through photographs taken under daylight and UV-light (λ= 365 nm). A comparison between the absence (blank) and presence of various metal ions highlights the distinctive quenching effect of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions on the PL intensity of PS-CDs. Remarkably, this effect is relatively weak compared to other carbon dots, underscoring the stable properties of PS-CDs. The observation that PS-CDs exhibit a weaker response to Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions compared to other carbon dots with -NH2-rich surfaces suggests the stable and unique properties of PS-CDs in metal ion sensing. This finding opens avenues for the application of PS-CDs as a selective and stable chemosensor for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, emphasizing their potential in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal experimental conditions, the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of PS-CDs was monitored at various Cu2+ concentrations. Fig. 7 a illustrates the PL spectra of PS-CDs with increasing Cu2+ concentrations (0 to 50 μM). Notably, the PL intensity of the formed PS-CDs/Cu2+ complex exhibited a consistent decrease corresponding to the elevated Cu2+ concentrations [50]. This visual change is further emphasized by the transformation of the emission color from bright blue to faint blue under UV irradiation, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 7a. The Jobs plot method was employed to ascertain the binding stoichiometry of the PS-CDs/Cu2+ complex, maintaining total concentrations at 50 μM. The molar ratio, determined by the mole fraction of [Cu2+], ranged from 0 to 1. Fig. 7b reveals that the maximum value of the molar ratio was 0.5, indicating a 1:1 binding stoichiometry for the PS-CDs/Cu2+ complex. To quantify the quenching efficiency (F0/F) through a Stern-Volmer (SV) plot, a linear correlation was established (R2 = 0.9950) over a concentration range of 0–50 μM, as shown in Fig. 7c [51]. Here, F0 and F represent the emission intensities of PS-CDs at 420 nm (λ max = 338 nm) in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively [52]. The linear regression equation is presented in Fig. 7d, illustrating the intensity profile of Cu2+ ions. Using the detection limit formula (K= SD/S), where S is the slope of the linear equation and SD is the standard deviation of the blank samples, the limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was determined to be 0.43 μM. Significantly, this LOD is lower than the World Health Organization maximum permitted concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water (2 mg/mL) [53], demonstrating the high sensitivity and selectivity of the Cu2+ detection system that has been developed. These results underscore the potential applicability of the proposed system in living cells, showcasing its promise for practical applications in metal ion sensing.

The mechanism of PL quenching by Fe3+ ions, the effect at different concentrations was systematically investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the PL intensity of PS-CDs exhibited a noticeable decrease with the Fe3+ ions concentration increasing from 0 to 50 μM [54]. Correspondingly, the emission color of PS-CDs shifted from bright blue to faint blue under UV irradiation, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 8a. Utilizing the Jobs plot method, the binding stoichiometry of the PS-CDs/Fe3+ complex was determined, maintaining total concentrations at 50 μM. The molar ratio, defined by the mole fraction of [Fe3+], ranged from 0 to 1. Fig. 8b indicates that the maximum value of the molar ratio was 0.5, representative a 1:1 binding stoichiometry for the PS-CDs/Fe3+ complex. The SV plot relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration demonstrated a good linear response over the range from 0 to 50 μM, with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9958) as shown in Fig. 8c. Here, F0 and F represent the emission intensity peak at 420 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively [55]. The nonlinear fitting of the entire range suggests the involvement of both dynamic and static quenching methods in the detection mechanism [56].

The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was determined to be 0.11 μM over the range from 0 to 25 μM (Fig. 8d). This low LOD indicates the high sensitivity of the sensor for Fe3+ ions. We establish that the limit of detection (LOD) for sensing Cu2+and Fe3+ ions with PS-CDs is 0.43 and 0.11 μM, which stands out favorably among various CDs [3,25,57–61] (Table S1). The proposed method for the detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ions using PL PS-CDs derived from pumpkin seeds offers several advantages. The synthesized through a hydrothermal method with pumpkin seeds as the carbon source, this environmentally friendly and cost-effective approach contrasts with conventional methods reliant on complex chemical processes and pricey starting materials. Notably, PS-CDs exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ions, enabling precise detection even amidst other interfering ions, vital for accurate analysis in complex sample matrices. With a low detection limit (LOD) surpassing World Health Organization recommendations for these ions in drinking water, the method ensures trace detection, essential for regulatory compliance. The straightforward synthesis process, compatible with basic laboratory equipment, further enhances their appeal. Additionally, their water solubility and stability facilitate handling and storage, while their unique optical properties, including robust PL and photostability, render them versatile materials applicable across sensor devices, imaging, and drug delivery systems. Overall, this method offers a dependable, cost-effective, and eco-friendly solution for Cu2+ and Fe3+ions detection, with wide-ranging applications spanning analytical chemistry, environmental monitoring, and biomedical research.

The comprehensive investigation into the quenching mechanism and the establishment of a robust sensing platform highlight the potential of PS-CDs for practical applications in detecting and quantifying Fe3+ ions. In order to comprehensively assess the selectivity of PS-CDs towards various metal ions, interference experiments were conducted to examine the impact of other metal ions on the binding of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions with PS-CDs (Fig. 9 ). The ratio of emission at 420 nm with 338 nm excitation, resulting from the combination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions with other metal ions mentioned above, exhibited similarity to that induced by Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions alone [61]. These consistent results strongly suggest that the recognition of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions by PS-CDs remained largely unaffected by the presence of other coexisting metal ions. Therefore, PS-CDs demonstrated remarkable selectivity towards Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in the tested conditions. This high selectivity is a crucial characteristic for practical applications, particularly in complex sample matrices where multiple metal ions may coexist. The ability of PS-CDs to selectively respond to specific metal ions, even in the existence of potential interfering ions, underscores their potential as robust and reliable chemosensors for targeted metal ion detection.

The sensing mechanisms for detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ions using PS-CDs likely involve complex interactions between the PS-CDs and the metal ions, leading to changes in PL intensity. These interactions are influenced by the presence of functional groups like carboxyl (-COOH), amino (-NH2), or hydroxyl (-OH) on the surface of PS-CDs, which serve as binding sites for the metal ions through coordination chemistry. Cu2+ and Fe3+ions, having a high affinity for electron-rich donor atoms such as oxygen and nitrogen, form strong coordination interactions with these functional groups. Both ions, being transition metals with partially filled d-orbitals, can readily form coordination complexes with ligands on the PS-CDs surface. The formation of these complexes involves electron pair donation from the functional groups on PS-CDs to the empty orbitals of Cu2+ and Fe3+ions, resulting in stable complexes. Additionally, the interaction may involve electron transfer processes, with Cu2+ and Fe3+ions acting as electron acceptors and PS-CDs as electron donors, potentially leading to the quenching of PL intensity. The arrangement and accessibility of functional groups on PS-CDs surface can provide selective binding sites for Cu2+ and Fe3+ions, making their coordination chemistry more favorable or specific compared to other metal ions. This selective binding can induce changes in surface charge density or distribution, affecting electrostatic interactions and contributing to the specificity of interactions between PS-CDs and metal ions. Overall, these factors, including surface functional groups, coordination chemistry, electron transfer processes, selective binding sites, and surface charge effects, govern the specific interactions between PS-CDs and Cu2+ and Fe3+, resulting in their high selectivity and sensitivity in metal ion sensing applications.

The nonlinear Stern-Volmer plot for PS-CDs in the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions indicates that the observed quenching in photoluminescence intensity may result from either a static mechanism or a combination of both static and dynamic mechanisms. The mechanism of quenching was assessed through photoluminescence lifetime measurements. In the static mechanism, quencher molecules bind to chemosensors in their ground state, forming a less fluorescent complex, while unbound molecules in solution retain their inherent lifetime. Conversely, in dynamic quenching, diffusion-controlled collisions between the excited state of the chemosensor and the quencher occur, leading to a significant decrease in the chemosensors lifetime. Therefore, time-resolved fluorescence spectra were acquired for representative complexes of PS-CDs/Cu2+ and PS-CDs/Fe3+ in acetonitrile solution (Fig. 10 a,b). The fluorescence lifetime spectrum of PS-CDs exhibited an exponential curve, with an average lifetime (τav) estimated at 16.82 ns. Furthermore, upon addition of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions to the PS-CDs solution, significant changes in the lifetime curve (τav) values were observed: 8.47 ns for Cu2+ ions and 3.61 ns for Fe3+ ions, respectively, strongly suggesting that quenching occurs via a static mechanism.

The potential of PS-CDs as biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials for cell imaging and labeling prompted an assessment of their toxicity in live-cell lines. The cytotoxicity of PS-CDs was evaluated using the MTT method on HCT-116 cells, with cells treated for 24 h at different concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μg/mL. As illustrated in Fig. 11 a, the viability test at various PS-CD concentrations up to 100 μg/mL revealed only a marginal decrease in viability, with a reduction of less than 8 % [62]. This result underscores the good biocompatibility of the synthesized PS-CDs, indicating their suitability for biological applications. In Fig. 11b, the cell cytotoxicity of the PS-CDs fluorescent probes was assessed in the context of cell imaging for Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions [63,64]. The biological cell imaging demonstrated confocal fluorescence images of HCT-116 cells treated with PS-CDs, PS-CDs/Cu2+, and PS-CDs/Fe3+ ions for 24 h. Under excitation at 338 nm, the HCT-116 cells emitted a strong blue fluorescence (PS-CDs) transitioning to faint blue emission (PS-CDs/Cu2+ and PS-CDs/Fe3+ ions) from the intracellular area [65–67]. This brightly illustrates that PS-CDs can traverse cell membranes and enter cells [68]. Hence, the low cytotoxicity and high potential of PS-CDs in bioimaging applications make them suitable for various in vitro and in vivo studies. These findings reinforce the promising biocompatibility of PS-CDs, positioning them as valuable candidates for safe and effective bioimaging applications, further expanding their utility in biological and medical research.

The functionality of PL PS-CDs obtained from pumpkin seeds was practically evaluated by detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in real spiked water samples, including drinking water, tap water, and poured water. The findings are summarized in Tables S2 and S3. Notably, the method demonstrated outstanding performance, yielding mean recovery rates ranging from 97.6 % to 103.4 %. Moreover, the relative standard deviations (RSD%) remained consistently below 2 %. These outcomes underscore the efficacy of PS-CDs derived from pumpkin seeds as promising candidates for the accurate assessment of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in real water samples.

In conclusion, a green and cost-effective hydrothermal method was developed to produce water-soluble photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots, utilizing pumpkin seeds as a carbon source. The resulting PS-CDs demonstrated excellent water solubility, high PL intensity, and robust photostability. Comprehensive characterization using various instrumental techniques, including UV–vis, PL, FT-IR, XRD, and HR-TEM analysis, confirmed the quality of the synthesized PS-CDs. The HR-TEM images revealed an average size of approximately 4.0 ± 0.2 nm, ranging from 2 to 7 nm with an interlayer distance of 0.21 nm. These ultra-small PS-CDs exhibited strong affinity, excellent selectivity, and remarkable sensing ability for Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. The Stern-Volmer plots for PS-CDs in the presence Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions demonstrated a linear response over the concentration range of 0–50 μM, with correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9950 for Cu2+ and 0.9958 for Fe3+. The binding stoichiometry of the PS-CDs/Cu2+ and PS-CDs/Fe3+ complexes was determined to be 1:1 using the Jobs plot method, indicating one metal ion binding to one PS-CD molecule. Moreover, the significant changes in fluorescence lifetime values were observed for PS-CDs in the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, indicating quenching via a static mechanism. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was determined to be 0.43 μM, while the LOD for Fe3+ was found to be 0.11 μM. These values were obtained using the detection limit formula (K = SD/S), and these values lower to the WHO recommendations for Cu2+ and Fe3+ ion concentrations in drinking water. Moreover, the small-sized PS-CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility, and good cell permeability. Consequently, the fluorescent PS-CDs probe successfully facilitated the imaging of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions, offering a valuable method for monitoring changes in the levels of these ions in living cells. The PS-CDs low cost, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and distinctive optical properties suggest numerous potential applications that can be explored with further research.
